‡ New York University ¶ Heidelberg University § University of Zurich

Abstract

We present Nematus, a toolkit for Neu- ral Machine Translation. The toolkit pri- oritizes high translation accuracy, usabil- ity, and extensibility. Nematus has been used to build top-performing submissions to shared translation tasks at WMT and IWSLT, and has been used to train systems for production environments.

bb06b15a-c82e-4db8-9dc3-5ac198830a79.text:
1 Introduction

Neural Machine Translation (NMT) (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Sutskever et al., 2014) has recently es- tablished itself as a new state-of-the art in machine translation. We present Nematus 1 , a new toolkit for Ne ural Ma chine T ranslation.Nematus has its roots in the dl4mt-tutorial.

These characteristics make it a good starting point for research in NMT. Nematus has been extended to include new func- tionality based on recent research, and has been used to build top-performing systems to last year’s shared translation tasks at WMT (Sennrich et al.

326a09c9-26a8-4b03-b2d8-7bddaaa1bbcb.text: , 2016) and IWSLT (Junczys-Dowmunt and Birch, 2016).Nematus is implemented in Python, and based on the Theano framework (Theano Develop- ment Team, 2016). It implements an attentional encoder–decoder architecture similar to Bahdanau et al. (2015).

1b2332af-6d8e-49c1-977b-80ed7edb5527.text:
Our neural network architecture dif- fers in some aspect from theirs, and we will dis- cuss differences in more detail.

 We will also de- scribe additional functionality, aimed to enhance
usability and performance, which has been imple- mented in Nematus.

2 Neural Network Architecture

Nematus implements an attentional encoder– decoder architecture similar to the one described by Bahdanau et al.

339ae5da-2039-450b-9233-dfd7dc7905b3.label: text_chunk
339ae5da-2039-450b-9233-dfd7dc7905b3.text:
(2015), but with several imple- mentation differences. The main differences are as follows:

• We initialize the decoder hidden state with the mean of the source annotation,

 rather than the annotation at the last position of the en- coder backward RNN.

• We implement a novel conditional GRU with attention.

• In the decoder,

 we use a feedforward hidden layer with tanh non-linearity rather than a maxout before the softmax layer.

523d9dd5-cdec-45ce-b0e3-dff13728c1a1.label: text_chunk
523d9dd5-cdec-45ce-b0e3-dff13728c1a1.text:
• In both encoder and decoder word embed- ding layers, we do not use additional biases.

• Compared to Look , Generate , Update de- coder phases in Bahdanau et al.

 (2015), we implement Look , Update , Generate which drastically simplifies the decoder implemen- tation (see Table 1).

• Optionally, we perform recurrent Bayesian dropout (Gal, 2015).

• Instead of a single word embedding at each source position,

0841063e-b343-4bbc-9cd5-29755f030528.text:
our input representations al- lows multiple features (or “factors”) at each time step, with the final embedding being the concatenation of the embeddings of each fea- ture (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016).1 available at https://github.com/rsennrich/nematus 2 https://github.com/nyu-dl/dl4mt-tutorial

A cGRU att uses its previous hidden state s j − 1 , the whole set of source annotations C = { h 1 , . . . , h T x } and the previously decoded symbol y j − 1 in order to update its hidden state s j , which is further used to decode symbol y j at position j ,
s j = cGRU att ( s j − 1 , y j − 1 , C )

548cdf16-516d-411c-92d3-79b62e44279b.text:
Our conditional GRU layer with attention mech- anism, cGRU att , consists of three components: two GRU state transition blocks and an attention mechanism ATT in between. The first transi- tion block, GRU 1 , combines the previous decoded
symbol y j − 1 and previous hidden state s j − 1 in or- der to generate an intermediate representation s 0 j with the following formulations:

r 0 j = σ W 0 r E [ y j − 1 ] + U 0 r s j − 1 , z 0 j = σ W 0 z E [ y j − 1 ] + U 0 z s j − 1 ,


where E is the target word embedding matrix, s 0 j is the proposal intermediate representation, r 0 j and z 0 j being the reset and update gate activations.

fe5aee5a-3355-4a68-b1b2-def92e3a50bb.text:
3 Training Algorithms

By default, the training objective in Nematus is cross-entropy minimization on a parallel training corpus.

35fe2736-7c75-4174-a5d9-7794b10fa1f6.label: text_chunk
35fe2736-7c75-4174-a5d9-7794b10fa1f6.text:
Training is performed via stochastic gra- dient descent, or one of its variants with adaptive learning rate (Adadelta (Zeiler, 2012), RmsProp (Tieleman and Hinton, 2012), Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014)).Additionally, Nematus supports minimum risk training (MRT) (Shen et al.

, 2016) to optimize to- wards an arbitrary, sentence-level loss function.

bc3bab50-375c-4f72-ab50-67b1f2cd9ee7.label: text_chunk
bc3bab50-375c-4f72-ab50-67b1f2cd9ee7.text:
Various MT metrics are supported as loss function, including smoothed sentence-level B LEU (Chen and Cherry, 2014), METEOR (Denkowski and Lavie, 2011), BEER (Stanojevic and Sima’an, 2014), and any interpolation of implemented met- rics.

To stabilize training, Nematus supports early stop- ping based on cross entropy, or an arbitrary loss function defined by the user.

7e521d7e-0b69-44e6-b484-739554fc0324.label: text_chunk
7e521d7e-0b69-44e6-b484-739554fc0324.text:
4 Usability Features

In addition to the main algorithms to train and decode with an NMT model, Nematus includes features aimed towards facilitating experimenta- tion with the models, and their visualisation. Var- ious model parameters are configurable via a command-line interface, and we provide extensive
Figure 1: Search graph visualisation for DE → EN translation of "Hallo Welt!" with beam size 3.documentation of options, and sample set-ups for training systems.

f98a9e7f-fd11-4fbd-925b-415a8cb40fbd.label: text_chunk
f98a9e7f-fd11-4fbd-925b-415a8cb40fbd.text: Nematus provides support for applying single models, as well as using multiple models in an en- semble – the latter is possible even if the model architectures differ, as long as the output vocabu- lary is the same. At each time step, the probability distribution of the ensemble is the geometric aver- age of the individual models’ probability distribu- tions. The toolkit includes scripts for beam search decoding, parallel corpus scoring and n-best-list rescoring.

2cf15416-c343-4d6c-8a0e-3a1c6543a3f4.label: text_chunk
2cf15416-c343-4d6c-8a0e-3a1c6543a3f4.text: Nematus includes utilities to visualise the atten- tion weights for a given sentence pair, and to vi- sualise the beam search graph. An example of the latter is shown in Figure 1. Our demonstration will cover how to train a model using the command- line interface, and showing various functionalities of Nematus, including decoding and visualisation, with pre-trained models.

5 Conclusion

We have presented Nematus, a toolkit for Neural Machine Translation. We have described imple- mentation differences to the architecture by Bah- danau et al.

4554dc5c-1c69-4fb8-8786-274f5501ca56.text:
(2015); due to the empirically strong performance of Nematus, we consider these to be of wider interest.We hope that researchers will find Nematus an ac- cessible and well documented toolkit to support

org/rsennrich/wmt16_systems/
their research.

92bf7e36-77ff-4938-91b7-1afcebc87b6d.label: text_chunk
92bf7e36-77ff-4938-91b7-1afcebc87b6d.text: The toolkit is by no means lim- ited to research, and has been used to train MT systems that are currently in production (WIPO, 2016).Nematus is available under a permissive BSD li- cense.

The relationships surrounding "Hate Speech" reveal its complex nature and the various contexts in which it operates:

1. **Legal Protection**: Hate speech is protected under the First Amendment in the United States, which guarantees freedom of speech, despite ongoing debates about its implications.

2. **Targeting**: Hate speech specifically targets certain groups, expressing hatred and derogatory sentiments aimed at members of those groups.

3. **Types and Examples**: 
   - The term "n*gger" is categorized as hate speech due to its historical context of racism and violence against African Americans.
   - The term "tranny" is also recognized as hate speech, used derogatorily against transgender individuals.
   - An anti-Chinese statement exemplifies hate speech by targeting a specific ethnic group.

4. **Classification and Analysis**: 
   - Hate speech is a subcategory of offensive speech, indicating that while all hate speech is offensive, not all offensive speech qualifies as hate speech.
   - It is classified using training data that includes examples of hateful language, aiding in the identification of hate speech instances.
   - Research, such as the study by Burnap and Williams, has analyzed tweets to identify occurrences of hate speech.

5. **Distinctions**: There is a critical distinction between hate speech and offensive language, which are often conflated. This distinction is important to avoid mislabeling individuals who may only be using offensive language rather than engaging in hate speech.

Overall, these relationships highlight the legal, social, and analytical dimensions of hate speech, emphasizing its targeted nature and the importance of careful classification and distinction in discussions surrounding it.

**Summary of Relationships Involving MagiCoder**

1. **Testing and Evaluation**: MagiCoder has been rigorously tested on a dataset of approximately 4500 manually revised reports to assess its performance against human revisions. Its effectiveness is further evaluated using benchmarks and performance metrics such as PT, which measures error minimization and recall precision.

2. **Functionality and Purpose**: Designed to assist pharmacologists and pharmacovigilance stakeholders, MagiCoder streamlines the encoding of Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) reports, significantly reducing the time required for this task. It processes narrative descriptions of suspected ADRs and generates relevant MedDRA terms, enhancing the efficiency of report revisions.

3. **Technological Integration**: MagiCoder utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms to support pharmacovigilance activities and is implemented as a plug-in for VigiFarmaco, extending its functionality. It maps Italian text to the MedDRA dictionary and recognizes terms within the LLT (Lowest Level Terms) framework.

4. **Collaboration and Development**: The software is developed in collaboration with pharmacovigilance experts and is part of a set of voted solutions, indicating its relevance in the coding community. It also collaborates with human experts to improve the coding of adverse events.

5. **User Interaction**: MagiCoder is designed for direct interaction with users, requiring quick responses to queries. It assists human experts by providing tools to identify redundancy errors, thereby enhancing decision-making in data encoding.

6. **Performance and Limitations**: The performance of MagiCoder is influenced by the complexities of the Italian language, particularly due to synonymical groups that affect coding accuracy. It contrasts with part-of-speech methodologies, as it does not consider sentence structure in its analysis.

7. **Future Enhancements**: Suggestions for improvement include integrating a spell checker feature from VigiFarmaco to enhance user input accuracy and reduce errors. Additionally, MagiCoder aims to test its procedures against various medical classification systems, including ICD-9.

Overall, MagiCoder represents a significant advancement in the automation of pharmacovigilance processes, enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of ADR reporting and coding through advanced technology and collaborative development.
